does: This paper introduces a massive multimodal preference dataset containing over 250 million human ratings across 2.2 million captions sourced from The New Yorker's cartoon caption contest over eight years. The authors develop novel benchmarks for evaluating humorous caption generation that combine GPT-4 and human judgments in ranking-based evaluation strategies. Through experiments with state-of-the-art models, they demonstrate that current preference-based fine-tuning methods like RLHF and DPO significantly underperform when applied to creative tasks, and that even advanced models like GPT-4 and Claude fall short of top human performers in generating funny captions. The work provides empirical evidence of the limitations of existing alignment techniques for subjective, creative domains.
connects: This work builds on the growing intersection of multimodal learning, creative AI generation, and preference-based fine-tuning methods. It extends prior research on large-scale preference datasets such as those used for training and evaluating language models, while adapting evaluation methodologies from creative tasks beyond traditional benchmarks like BLEU or ROUGE. The paper responds to recent advances in preference learning algorithms including RLHF and DPO, which have shown success in general language modeling but lack evaluation on nuanced creative tasks. It also connects to earlier work on automated humor detection and generation, as well as multimodal understanding combining vision and language. The use of crowdsourced contest data as a preference signal builds on established practices of leveraging real-world human feedback rather than artificial preference construction.
enables: This large-scale preference dataset enables development of specialized models for creative content generation with genuine human preference signals, moving beyond traditional metrics that struggle with subjective quality. The work opens pathways for designing new fine-tuning algorithms specifically suited to creative tasks where standard preference-learning methods fail, potentially requiring different architectural choices or reward modeling approaches. It establishes humor generation as a concrete benchmark domain for evaluating multimodal model capabilities and alignment techniques, encouraging more nuanced evaluation frameworks beyond standard NLU metrics. Future research can investigate why preference-based methods underperform on creative tasks and develop novel training strategies accordingly. The dataset also enables cross-modal creativity research and provides a testbed for exploring how large models might be better calibrated to human aesthetic and comedic preferences in ways that respect the subjective nature of humor.
